topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The morning light, usually a cheerful intrusion, felt heavy and oppressive as I reached for my textbook. Something was wrong. My journal, usually tucked beneath my pillow, was gone. I patted the bedside table frantically, my fingers brushing against cold wood. A cold knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, unwelcome clenching. Then I saw it—a faint, almost imperceptible smudge of ink on the corner of my diary, resting on the edge of my desk. My breath hitched. I knew, with a sickening certainty, that Liam had been reading it. His eyes, usually so bright, were currently fixed on the page, a strange, unreadable expression playing on his face. The silence in the room amplified the frantic thumping in my own ears. I just stared at him, my throat suddenly dry, unable to form a coherent protest or accusation.

[story 2]

Every time I walked into the shared bathroom, the sound of running water seemed amplified, mocking the quiet ache settling deep in my core. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting to see him, Liam, but he was always there, engrossed in whatever book he held. Today, though, something shifted. I noticed the slight misalignment of his posture, the way his gaze lingered a fraction too long on my side of the room. A tremor ran through my hands as I noticed the familiar spiral of my private thoughts, spilled onto the page. My stomach twisted into a tight, hollow space. When he finally looked up, his expression was unreadable, a mask of quiet contemplation that made my skin crawl. "Everything alright?" he asked, his voice level, devoid of inflection. I managed a weak nod, my gaze dropping instantly to my worn sneakers, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted across the floorboards, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air, and the stillness of the apartment felt vast and isolating. I was trying to focus on my assignment, but my attention kept snagging on the desk. My journal was open, the handwriting familiar, yet somehow invaded. A wave of profound weariness washed over me, making my eyelids feel impossibly heavy. When I finally dared to look up, Liam was sitting on the edge of his bed, a book resting open in his lap. He wasn't looking at me; he was looking down, his focus intense. The air thickened, pressing down on my lungs. I felt a desperate urge to retreat, to become invisible. When he finally shifted, his eyes met mine for a fleeting second—a flicker of something I couldn't decipher—and I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to just dissolve into the worn fabric of my chair.
